staircases.caption: A clean built flight: low, wide treads fronted by a kerb of round stones. The kind I counted without hesitation.
A stepped stone ramp paved with rounded cobbles — more incline than staircase, but the risers are real.
Big irregular blocks making a few deep steps. Every one a different size — counted, but a headache to count.
A rough, narrow flight climbing through cloud forest — uneven enough that two people might count it differently.
The long climb: dozens of uneven risers vanishing into the mist — with the count notebook in the corner of the frame.

paving.caption: Cobbled paving from above — flat enough that it's a path, not a stair.
A gentle cobbled ramp. Some risers, but mostly walking grade.
Rounded cobbles set into the slope — the boundary case between step and path.
Paved trail through the cloud forest. Counted only where a real riser showed up.
Neat dry-stone paving. Lovely work, but flat — these don't add to the count.

notSteps.caption: A single stone bar laid across the dirt. One lone edging stone — not a flight, didn't count it.
Loose rocky scramble. Stones underfoot, but nothing built — not steps.
Rounded stones half-buried in a dirt ramp. Worn past the point of being a step.
Flat dirt with one faint buried stone. The kind of thing I deliberately did not count.

counting.caption: The overlook above the Urubamba — notebook and boot in the frame, mid-count.
The day's tallies, held up against the peaks.
Another page of the running count, on the way to the pass.
Pages dense with tallies — every flight, section by section.
The count over a vast morning valley near the end of the trail.
Counting in the rain near the high pass, paper in hand.
Cloud, drizzle, and a stubborn tally that day.
Walking and counting in the rain — a few seconds from the trail.
